scenario: A team's User Task collects free-text feedback. The product owner asks if assignees can format text -- bold, lists, links -- in the form field.
question: Does Camunda Forms support rich-text input fields?

options.text: Camunda Forms has always included a native rich-text editor input component in every version, allowing assignees to use bold text, lists, and links in their submitted form responses. This overstates current Forms capabilities. 8, the standard Camunda Forms input components include Textfield and Textarea, both of which store plain text. There is no built-in WYSIWYG rich-text input component in the standard Forms library. Rich-text display is possible via the Text view component (Markdown rendering), but that is read-only, not user input.

Camunda Forms will never support rich-text input fields of any kind and is permanently limited to plain-text Textfield and Textarea components. This is too absolute. 8 does not ship a native rich-text editor input component, the Forms library evolves with each Camunda release. Additionally, workarounds exist -- such as training users to write Markdown in Textarea fields and rendering the output via a downstream Text view component or custom front-end renderer. Future versions may introduce a dedicated rich-text or HTML editor input component.

Camunda Forms has no text input components at all and relies entirely on selection-based controls such as dropdowns, checkboxes, and radio buttons for user interaction. This misrepresents the documented behavior. Camunda Forms includes both Textfield (single-line text input) and Textarea (multi-line text input) as standard input components. Additionally, the Text view component renders read-only Markdown or FEEL expression output. The Forms component library also includes Number, Date, Select, Checkbox, Radio, and other types. Textfield supports validation constraints including required, minimum length, maximum length, and regular expression patterns for email and phone.

8. md:3, validation includes Required + Regular-expression pattern incl. Email/Phone/Custom per :23-26 + Min/Max length per :24-25) and Textarea (multi-line). }} templating + FEEL =... expressions) -- read-only, not user input. md) similar but raw HTML. For rich-text INPUT, workarounds: (1) Textarea + Markdown convention -- train users to type Markdown, render downstream via Text view or external renderer; (2) External rich-text editor (TinyMCE / Quill / TipTap) on a custom front-end, sync result string into a Camunda variable via the Tasklist REST API; (3) Structured form pattern -- replace free-form rich text with multiple typed fields (subject, body, attachments) that compose downstream.

a.text: Incorrect. Camunda Forms text-input options: Standard text components (typically available): Text Field: single-line plain text. Text Area: multi-line plain text. Both store as plain string variables. Rich-text formatting (verify per Camunda 8 / Forms version): Some versions may add a Rich Text or HTML editor component. Output: HTML / Markdown string in process variable. Modeler-side: WYSIWYG toolbar for bold / italic / links / lists. If natively unavailable, workarounds: Pattern 1: Markdown convention: Use a Text Area component. Train users to write Markdown syntax (bold, * list item, link). Downstream renderer (custom web view) renders Markdown to HTML. Pros: works with any plain-text field; cross-platform. Cons: users need Markdown knowledge. Pattern 2: HTML in textarea: User writes HTML directly (advanced users only). Risk: XSS if not sanitised downstream. Pattern 3: External rich editor: Build custom front-end with TinyMCE / Quill / etc. Sync result to Camunda via API. Most flexibility; most engineering work. Pattern 4: Pre-defined templates: Form provides structured fields (subject, body sections) instead of free-form rich text. Compose final document from structured fields. Reduces need for rich formatting. Best practice: For most workflows, plain text + Markdown convention suffices. For polished customer-facing documents, external editors more appropriate. Always check current Forms component catalog -- features evolve.

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
